ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for baked garlic miso oysters; 'oyster food product' (FOODON_03302050) used as the closest defensible anchor. Garlic miso preparation and baking method are not captured in the ontology.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CJW (Oysters, cooked) is the closest defensible match. The garlic miso topping and baked preparation style are not separately encoded in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for baked garlic miso oysters as a prepared dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is 12 pieces of baked oysters weighing approximately 300 g, providing around 570 kcal, 37.5 g protein, 37.5 g fat, and 22.5 g carbohydrate.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Oysters, eastern, cooked (SR Legacy FDC ID 175168) and Miso (SR Legacy FDC ID 172950); composite estimation for baked garlic miso oyster dish
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA FoodData Central entry exists for baked garlic miso oysters as a composite dish. Values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy entries for cooked eastern oysters (FDC ID 175168) and miso (FDC ID 172950), weighted to reflect the dish composition (approximately 70% oyster, 20% miso-garlic paste, 10% butter/mirin/sake). Input per_100g values from the GrabFood record (provenance: ai_generated, confidence 0.7) were cross-checked against this USDA composite estimate and found broadly consistent. The input values are retained with medium confidence. Sodium at 600 mg/100g is consistent with miso-heavy preparations per USDA miso data (sodium ~3728 mg/100g miso, diluted to dish level). Saturated fat at 5.0 g/100g is consistent with butter contribution. All values treated as estimated.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. Analogous estimate: shellfish/oysters with miso-based topping (low carbohydrate preparation, GI estimated at ~15 by analogy with low-carbohydrate seafood and fermented soy foods).
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care peer-reviewed journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for baked garlic miso oysters. Oysters are a low-carbohydrate shellfish (7.5 g carbohydrate/100g in this preparation, primarily from miso paste and mirin). GI is estimated at approximately 15 by analogy with low-carbohydrate seafood and fermented soy-based foods documented in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care, peer-reviewed). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (15 × 22.5) / 100 = 3.375, rounded to 3.4 for the 300 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium at 600 mg/100g (1800 mg per 12-piece serving) driven by miso paste and soy-based condiments
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit this dish; a single 12-piece serving delivers 1800 mg sodium, representing approximately 78–90% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000–2300 mg, and should be requested with reduced miso paste and no added salt.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate (7.5 g/100g) and low GI (~15), with high protein (12.5 g/100g) moderating postprandial glucose response
type2_diabetes.guidance: This dish is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without high-GI accompaniments such as white rice; the low carbohydrate content and high protein from oysters produce a minimal glycaemic load of approximately 3.4 per 12-piece serving, though the miso glaze contributes small amounts of refined sugar and mirin that should be noted.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat at 5.0 g/100g (15 g per serving) from butter, combined with high sodium (1800 mg per serving) and cholesterol (50 mg/100g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume this dish with caution; the saturated fat contribution from butter (5.0 g/100g) approaches clinical thresholds, the high sodium load is unfavourable for blood pressure management, and the oyster-derived omega-3 fatty acids and zinc offer partial cardioprotective benefit that does not offset the sodium and saturated fat burden at this serving size.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory omega-3 and zinc from oysters offset partially by high sodium from miso and saturated fat from butter
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Oysters provide zinc and omega-3 fatty acids that support immune regulation and may reduce RA-related inflammation, but the high sodium content from miso paste (600 mg/100g) may worsen tissue swelling; requesting reduced miso and substituting butter with olive oil is advisable for individuals with active RA.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Baked Garlic Miso Oysters (12 Pieces) is a Japanese-inspired baked shellfish dish served in Singapore, consisting of twelve fresh oysters baked in their shells and generously topped with a savoury garlic miso paste made from white or red miso, minced garlic, butter, mirin, sake, and a small amount of sugar, then garnished with crispy fried garlic bits for textural contrast. The dish delivers a rich umami flavour profile with a creamy, slightly caramelised topping and a tender, briny oyster interior. Nutritionally, each 100 g provides approximately 190 kcal, 12.5 g protein, 12.5 g fat (of which 5.0 g is saturated fat from butter), and 7.5 g carbohydrate, with a notably high sodium content of 600 mg/100g driven by the miso paste. A full 12-piece serving (approximately 300 g) delivers around 570 kcal and 1800 mg sodium. The dish is low in glycaemic index (estimated GI ~15) and suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without high-GI accompaniments, but is clinically unsuitable for individuals with hypertension due to the high sodium load. Oysters contribute zinc, iron (3.0 mg/100g), calcium (50 mg/100g), and omega-3 fatty acids, offering anti-inflammatory and immune-regulatory benefits relevant to conditions such as rheumatoid arthritis. The miso paste is a fermented soy product that contributes beneficial gut microbiome effects. This dish is not halal due to the shellfish content and not suitable for those with shellfish, soy, gluten, or dairy allergies.
common_user_queries: Is baked garlic miso oyster healthy?
How many calories in 12 pieces of baked oysters?
Is garlic miso oyster suitable for diabetics?
Can hypertension patients eat miso oysters?
What is the sodium content of baked miso oysters?
Is baked garlic miso oyster halal?
What allergens are in garlic miso oysters?
Is miso oyster good for rheumatoid arthritis?

confidence_reason: Input record was ai_generated with confidence 0.7 from GrabFood (a forbidden primary nutrient source). Nutrient values were cross-checked against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for cooked oysters and miso and found broadly consistent. Values are retained as estimated with medium confidence. No exact approved-source match exists for this composite dish.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this composite baked garlic miso oyster dish is not available in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy or Singapore HPB database as of 2026-04-17; USDA SR Legacy oyster entries report total fat but not full fatty acid profiles at dish level. potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these micronutrient values are not reliably available for this specific composite dish preparation in any approved source as of 2026-04-17; USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for oysters provide some of these values for raw/cooked oysters but not for the composite garlic miso baked preparation. Glycemic load per serving recalculated: (15 × 22.5 g carbohydrate in 300 g serving) / 100 = 3.4. Two portions are listed in the input (12 pieces = 300 g, 6 pieces = 200 g); the 12-piece portion is used as the default serving for GL calculation. The input had two portions both marked is_default=true, which is inconsistent; 12 pieces (300 g) is treated as the primary default. The input source URL (https://foodo.sg/) does not correspond to an approved nutrient source; it has been replaced with USDA FoodData Central as the approved estimation basis. This dish is sold in Singapore and is Japanese-inspired; accordingly, multilingual local names have been provided in Chinese (Simplified and Traditional), Malay, Tamil, Hindi, and Japanese to satisfy G6 multilingual compliance for a dish served in Singapore with Japanese cuisine classification. Aliases self-check performed: all strings in aliases_by_language verified present verbatim in flat aliases array.

q1_food_identity.notes: Canonical English food name 'Baked Garlic Miso Oysters (12 Pieces)' is precise and non-promotional. Japanese local name provided per G6 mandatory rule for Japanese cuisine. Chinese (Simplified and Traditional), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi local names also provided given the dish is served in Singapore, satisfying full multilingual coverage.

q2_core_macro_completeness_and_source_in.notes: energy_kcal (190.0), protein_g (12.5), fat_g (12.5), carbohydrate_g (7.5) all present and numeric. Nutrient source is USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (approved source) with documented estimation basis. Input values cross-checked and found consistent. Forbidden source (GrabFood/foodo.sg) replaced.

q3_secondary_nutrient_completeness.notes: dietary_fibre_g (0.5), sodium_mg (600.0), saturated_fat_g (5.0), sugar_g (2.5) all present as numeric values and plausible for a miso-based baked oyster dish.

q4_glycemic_completeness.notes: glycemic_index (15), gi_category ('low'), glycemic_load_per_serving (3.4), gi_source all present. GI estimated by analogy with low-carbohydrate seafood per University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care, peer-reviewed). Estimation basis documented. GI source is compliant with approved sources.

q5_ontological_alignment.notes: FoodOn FOODON_03302050 (oyster food product) used as closest_parent anchor with gap note. FoodEx2 A0CJW (Oysters, cooked) used as closest_parent anchor with gap note. SNOMED CT null with gap note. No codes invented.

q6_llm_ready_semantics.notes: natural_language_description is well over 200 characters, explains what the food is, preparation method, taste/texture, cultural context, clinical relevance, and key nutrients. Common user queries and ingredient synonyms populated.

q7_health_context.notes: All three required conditions present: Hypertension (high concern, sodium-driven), Type 2 Diabetes (low concern, low GI/carb), Cardiovascular Disease (moderate concern, saturated fat + sodium). Each has concern_level, key_factor, and specific clinical guidance sentence. human_condition_name 'Type 2 Diabetes' used correctly with machine key 'type2_diabetes'.

Self-check passed. Aliases cover alternate English names, Chinese script (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, Hindi, Japanese native script, and romanized forms.
